segments.s014-g41: جهان (subject) begins the line. سر به سر is an idiomatic duplicative phrase meaning “entirely/utterly” (literally “head to head”); چُو is the simile marker “like/as,” and فسانست is the poetic contraction of فسانه است / فسانی است meaning “is a fable” (فسان + است fused). بس at the end is a final particle meaning “enough/that is all” or “indeed,” wrapping up the clause. Word order is subject — adverbial idiom — simile marker — predicate — concluding particle, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s014-g42: نماند is negative third‑person singular meaning “does not remain / is not left” (ن + ماند); here it functions as the main verb. بد و نیک are coordinated nouns/adjectives “bad and good.” بر هیچ‌کس is a prepositional phrase marking the experiencer/possessor (“for/on no one”). The clause order places the verb before its complements (poetic inversion), yielding “(there) remains not bad and good for anyone” → “neither bad nor good remains for anyone.”
segments.s014-g17: پری و پلنگ انجمن کرد: a compound subject (پری و پلنگ) comes before the past-tense verb کرد (literally 'made/ did'), where انجمن (literally 'assembly') functions as the event (انجمن کردن = 'to assemble'). The trailing و شیر is joined by و to continue the list of participants — the conjunction attaches after the verb to add another noun ('and a lion').
segments.s014-g18: ز درندگان گرگ و ببر دلیر: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a subgroup or source ('of the predators'); درندگان is the genitive/collective 'predators.' The list that follows (گرگ و ببر) names members of that group and دلیر is a postposed adjective 'brave' modifying ببر (tiger).
segments.s014-g19: سپاهی دد و دام و مرغ و پری: سپاهی ('a host/army') is head noun followed by a coordinated list (دد، دام، مرغ و پری) in apposition that specifies what the host consists of. The repeated و is the standard coordinating conjunction; the list mixes animate and inanimate elements as a poetic catalogue.
segments.s014-g20: سپهدار پرکین و کنداوری: سپهدار ('commander', a title) is followed by two coordinated proper names (پرکین و کنداوری) that serve as its apposition/identification; word order is Title + Names, typical for naming leaders.
segments.s014-g21: پس پشت لشکر کیومرث شاه: پس 'then' gives temporal sequence; پشت لشکر 'the rear/behind of the army' places the action spatially, and the following names کیومرث شاه are in genitive relation to لشکر (the army of King Keyumarth). Word order is temporal adverb → locative phrase → possessor.
segments.s014-g22: نبیره به پیش اندرون با سپاه: نبیره ('scion/offspring') is the subject; به پیش اندرون is a directional phrase 'forward/into the interior' (به + پیش/اندرون), and با سپاه 'with an army' is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase. The verb is omitted here (a common poetic ellipsis), so the clause implies that the scion moved/advanced with the army.
segments.s014-g23: بیامد سیه دیو با ترس و باک: بیامد is the simple past 'came'; سیه دیو is the nominal subject 'the black demon.' با ترس و باک is a doubled prepositional phrase 'with fear and with dread' (با repeated for emphasis), describing the demon's state as it came.
segments.s014-g24: همی بآسمان بر پراگند خاک: همی is a frequentative/emphatic particle ('repeatedly/continually'); بآسمان ('to the sky', the prefixed ب- + آسمان) and بر here indicate direction/'toward/upwards', and پراگند خاک is the object phrase 'scattered earth/dust' (پراگند modifying خاک). Together they mean he flung/scattered earth up into the sky.
segments.s014-g1: خجسته is an epithet placed before the name (poetic adjective meaning 'blessed/auspicious'). سیامک is the proper name (subject). یکی پور is a quantified noun phrase ('one son' / 'a certain son') with یکی before پور. داشت is the past-tense verb 'had' (possession) and comes at the end in typical Persian SOV order.
segments.s014-g2: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this clause to the son. نزد + نیا is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the ancestor' (نزد = 'in the presence of', نیا = 'ancestor'). جاه دستور is a nominal compound meaning 'rank and authority / status and command'. داشت is again the past 'had', agreeing with the relative 'که'.
segments.s014-g3: گرانمایه is a noun/adjective 'the esteemed/precious one'. را here is the definite object/topic marker attaching to گرانمایه (poetic word order places it before the copular clause). نام هوشنگ بود is a copular clause 'the name was Hûshang' (نام = 'name', هوشنگ = proper name, بود = past 'was').
segments.s014-g4: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you' (vocative/addresser). گفتی is the past form 'you said' (here introducing reported speech or an aside); grammatically it governs the following predicative clause. همه هوش و فرهنگ بود is a nominal predicate 'he was entirely intellect and culture' (همه = 'entirely/all', هوش = 'intellect', و = 'and', فرهنگ = 'culture/refinement', بود = past copula). Note: in the provided lemma senses, گفتی was listed as conversation-related nouns rather than the verb; the correct grammatical role here is the verb 'you said'.
segments.s014-g5: به نزد is a prepositional phrase 'in the sight/presence of'. نیا is the object 'the ancestor'. یادگار پدر is a genitive phrase 'the father's memento/legacy' (یادگار = 'memento/legacy', پدر = 'father'), describing the son's status in the ancestor's eyes.
segments.s014-g6: This line is poetically compressed: نیا پروریده is a past-participial idea 'reared by the ancestor' or 'the ancestor had reared (him)'. مر is an archaic/dialectal dative 'to me' (used before the object for emphasis). او را is the direct object 'him' (را = object marker). به بر is a locative phrase 'to (or on) the bosom/arm' (بر here = 'upon/to the breast'); the clause therefore says the ancestor reared him and placed him to my bosom. Word order and omission of an explicit auxiliary are typical of epic/poetic compression.
segments.s014-g7: نیایش is 'prayer/devotion'. به جای is an idiom 'in place of / instead of' (به + جای). پسر is 'son' as the noun in that idiom. داشتی is the second-person past 'you had' (or 'you kept/held'), so the whole phrase means 'you had prayer instead of a son.'
segments.s014-g8: جز او = 'except him'. بر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'upon anyone' (بر = 'on/upon', کسی = 'anyone/someone'). چشم نگماشتی is a negative past: چشم = 'eye/gaze' and نگماشتی (poetic past form from گماشتن with negative prefix ن-) = 'you did not place/set' — i.e. 'you did not cast your gaze' or 'you looked to no one'. The negation is integrated into نگماشتی, so the full phrase = 'you did not look to anyone except him.'
segments.s014-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنهاد is the archaic past form of نهادن (to put/instill) in 3rd singular, so the clause means 'when he instilled/put'. دل کینه و جنگ را is the object phrase: دل 'heart' is the locus, کینه و جنگ 'hatred and war' are the items instilled, and را marks the definite object. Word order is typical Classical Persian (conjunction + verb + object phrase).
segments.s014-g10: بخواند here means 'he summoned/called' (an older poetic use of خواندن meaning 'to call/summon') with the verb in past/performative position. آن گرانمایه هوشنگ را is the definite object: آن 'that/the', گرانمایه an epithet 'esteemed/precious', هوشنگ the name, and را marks the object. The verb precedes the object for poetic rhythm but is syntactically transitive.
segments.s014-g11: همه گفتنی‌ها is the subject phrase 'all the sayable things / everything that should be said'. بدو is the indirect object 'to him'. بازگفت is the past tense verb 'recounted / told again'. Order is subject + indirect object + verb (SOV) as in classical Persian.
segments.s014-g12: همه رازها is the subject 'all the secrets'. بر گشاد functions as a verbal phrase 'opened up / disclosed' (بر here works with گشاد to indicate the action of opening/unveiling). از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from concealment / out of hiding'. Together they mean he revealed all the secrets that had been hidden.
segments.s014-g13: که introduces a reported or purposive clause here ('that' / 'for'). من لشکری کرد خواهم همی contains a split verb construction: کرد (root 'to make') plus خواهم (the future auxiliary 'I will'), with همی an archaic emphatic/verbal particle. Together they yield 'I will make/raise an army.' لشکری is the object 'an army / troops.'
segments.s014-g14: خروشی is the direct object 'a roar / outcry'. برآورد plus the auxiliary خواهم (and particle همی) is another split-verb future construction meaning 'will produce/raise' (here: 'raise a cry'). The order verb-stem + auxiliary is typical in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s014-g15: تو را here uses را as a topical/emphatic marker after the pronoun ('for you' / 'you,'). بود باید is a conventional classical construction meaning 'it was necessary' or 'ought to be'; together with پیشرو ('vanguard / leader') the clause means 'you ought to be the vanguard.' همی is an archaic emphatic particle attached to the predicate.
segments.s014-g16: که here introduces the reason 'because / for'. من رفتنی‌ام uses رفتنی (about to go / departing) with the copular suffix -ام 'I am', so 'I am about to leave.' تو سالار نو is a nominal predicate 'you (are) the new commander' with the copula omitted (a common classical construction); context supplies the sense 'you will be the new lord/commander.'
segments.s014-g33: This clause uses the classical word order with the prepositional phrase first: 'به پای اندر افگند' (he threw [him] into/at the feet). 'به' governs 'پای' ('to/at the foot[s]'), 'اندر' is the older preposition 'into/within' placed after the object, and 'افگند' is the past-tense perfective of the verb 'افکندن' (threw). The conjunction 'و' connects the next verb phrase; 'بسپرد' is the past (perfective) of 'سپردن' (handed over/entrusted) and is followed by the adjective 'خوار' (humiliated/despised) used predicatively to mark the manner in which he was handed over.
segments.s014-g34: 'دریده بر او چرم' places the adjective 'دریده' (torn) before the noun 'چرم' (leather/jerkin) with the preposition 'بر' meaning 'on/upon' and the pronoun 'او' (him) as the experiencer ('the leather torn upon him'). The coordinating 'و' links to the short clause 'برگشته کار' in which 'برگشته' is a participial/adjectival past form meaning 'turned back/reverted' describing 'کار' (the matter/affair) — a compact poetic way to say the affair was turned back or reversed.
segments.s014-g35: The clause begins with the temporal conjunction 'چو' (when), followed by the past verb 'آمد' (came). The clitic/pronoun 'مر' here means 'to me' (the dative clitic attached as a separate word in older usage). 'آن کینه را خواستار' is a noun phrase 'that grudge seeking' lit. 'that-grudge the-seeker'—'را' is the object/topic marker and 'خواستار' functions as an adjective/agent noun 'seeking/claimant,' so the whole phrase reads 'when that grudge came seeking me.'
segments.s014-g36: This short clause puts 'سرآمد' (conclusion/end) before the name 'کیومرث' with the object marker 'را' following the name, a somewhat inverted classical order meaning 'the end came for Kiumarṯ.' 'روزگار' here denotes the 'time/era' or 'fate' that consummates that end. In other words, the phrase means 'the time/fate of Kiumarṯ reached its conclusion.'
segments.s014-g37: The verb 'برفت' (classical past of 'رفتن', 'he went/left') begins the clause (an archaic past form often written as one word). 'و جهان مردری ماند ازوی' follows with 'جهان' (the world) as topic, 'مردری' (manliness/valor) as the thing that 'ماند' (remained/was left), and 'ازوی' ('from him') indicating the source. The overall order emphasizes the remaining legacy ('a man's valor remained in the world from him').
segments.s014-g38: 'نگر' is an imperative 'look!' or 'behold!'. The particle 'تا' here functions as a coordinator meaning roughly 'so/that' leading into a relative/interrogative construction 'که را' ('که' = who/which, with the object marker 'را' emphasizing the person). 'نزد او آبروی' places 'نزد' (in the presence of) before the pronoun 'او' and the noun 'آبروی' (honor/reputation) to ask rhetorically 'to whom before him is any honor left?' The clause is archaic word order typical of epic poetry.
segments.s014-g39: Here 'جهان فریبنده را' is a noun phrase 'the deceiving/deceptive world' marked by the object/topic marker 'را', with 'گرد کرد' (lit. 'made round/about' or 'turned into') forming a causative/perfective construction with 'کرد' as the light verb. In this poetic compressed phrasing, the effect is 'he (or fate) made the world deceptive' or 'the world was made into a deceiver.'
segments.s014-g40: 'ره سود بنمود' is a classical phrase: 'ره' (the way/path), 'سود' (profit/advantage), and 'بنمود' (showed/displayed) together mean 'it showed the path of profit.' The conjunction 'و' links the next concise clause 'خود مایه خورد' where 'خود' ('itself/he himself') is the subject, 'مایه' (capital/resource/substance) is the object, and 'خورد' (ate/consumed) is the past verb — idiomatically 'and it himself consumed the capital,' i.e. the promised profit turned out to devour the principal.
segments.s014-g25: ز introduces a cause or source ('from'); هرای درندگان is a genitive-like phrase where هرای (an archaic/poetic form meaning 'fear/panic') is followed by درندگان ('predators'), so together 'the panic/fear of the predators'; چنگ دیو is a noun+noun sequence (poetic omission of the ezāfe) meaning 'the demon's claw/talon' with the possessor (دیو) following the possessed (چنگ).
segments.s014-g26: شده is the perfect/passive-like participle 'has become' (past/result state); سست is the predicate adjective 'weak'; از marks source/reason ('from, out of'); خشم کیهان is a genitive/compound 'the anger of the cosmos' (کیهان = universe); خدیو follows as a title/appositive 'the ruler' (poetic/archaic title) modifying whose anger is meant. The whole segment expresses a resulting state caused by that anger.
segments.s014-g27: به هم is an adverbial phrase here meaning 'together' (به with هم); برشکستند is a past plural verb 'they were broken/scattered' (برشکستن); هر دو گروه is a disambiguating noun phrase 'both groups' placed after the verb for poetic word order, repeating/emphasizing the subject.
segments.s014-g28: شدند is the past plural of شدن used here as 'they became'; از introduces cause ('from'); دد و دام lists the causes 'enemy/hostility (دد) and trap/snares (دام)'; دیوان in the poem functions as the plural/collective 'demons' (an older/poetic plural of دیو) — this sense is not among the provided dictionary senses, so it is the semantic reading here; ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary/sick of'. The phrase means 'they became weary from the enemies and the demons' traps.'
segments.s014-g29: بیازید is a poetic past/preterite meaning 'he sprang forth/broke out' (from یاختن/یاختن) describing sudden motion; هوشنگ is the subject name; چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; شیر چنگ is a compound simile 'a lion's talon/claw' (شیر = lion, چنگ = claw/talon) with noun+noun order typical in poetry.
segments.s014-g30: جهان کرد is a causative construction: جهان ('the world') + کرد ('made/did'), an idiomatic poetic turn meaning 'he caused things upon the world' or 'he pressed/acted so that the world [felt it]'; بر دیو sets the target 'against/upon the demon' (بر = against/upon); نستوه is a postpositive adjective 'distressed/vexed'; تنگ is another adjective 'tight/close' describing the pressure applied. Together it conveys he pressed the demon tightly/distressed him severely.
segments.s014-g31: کشیدش is the past tense verb 'he pulled/dragged' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('him/it'); سراپای (سراپا) and یک‌سر are emphatic adverbial phrases meaning 'from head to foot' and 'entirely/utterly' respectively and follow the verb for emphasis in poetic order; دوال is an archaic/poetic qualifier ('twofold/paired' or reinforcing completeness) that further intensifies the sense of totality here.
segments.s014-g32: سپهبد is the agent/title 'the spahbed' placed before the verb (typical subject–verb order); برید is the past of بریدن 'he cut/severed' (here 'severed/cut off'); آن سر is the direct object 'that head' with آن as demonstrative; بی‌همال is a postpositive adjective meaning 'without equal / matchless' (poetic epithet) qualifying the head. The clause states that the spahbed severed that peerless head.

sentences.s014-s21: The world is altogether like a fable, and neither bad nor good remains for anyone.
sentences.s014-s9: A fairy and a panther gathered together, and a lion; among the predators were a wolf and a brave tiger.
sentences.s014-s10: A host of foes, traps, birds, and fairies; the commanders were the sepahdars Parkin and Kandauri.
sentences.s014-s11: Then, at the rear of King Keyumarth's army, the scion advanced inward with troops.
sentences.s014-s12: The black demon came, with fear and dread, and repeatedly hurled earth toward the sky.
sentences.s014-s1: The blessed Siyâmak had a son who, in the presence of the ancestor, held rank and authority.
sentences.s014-s2: That esteemed one was named Hûshang; you say (you have said) he was all intellect and refinement.
sentences.s014-s3: In the ancestor's sight he was his father's memento; the ancestor had reared him and placed him to (on) my bosom.
sentences.s014-s4: You had prayer in the stead of a son; except for him you did not set your eyes upon anyone.
sentences.s014-s5: When he planted hatred and the desire for war in his heart, he summoned that esteemed Hushang.
sentences.s014-s6: He recounted to him all that could be said, and laid open all the secrets that had been hidden.
sentences.s014-s7: That I will raise an army I will; I will raise a shout I will.
sentences.s014-s8: You ought to be the vanguard, for I am about to go; you are the new commander.
sentences.s014-s17: He cast him at his feet and handed him over in contempt; his leather was torn upon him and the matter was reversed.
sentences.s014-s18: When that grudge came seeking me, the era of Kiumarṯ reached its end.
sentences.s014-s19: He departed, and a legacy of manly valor remained in the world from him; look, see to whom any honor remains in his presence.
sentences.s014-s20: He made the world deceitful; it showed the path of profit and itself consumed the capital.
sentences.s014-s13: From the dread caused by the predators' claws, the demon's talon became weak through the anger of the cosmic ruler.
sentences.s014-s14: Both groups were shattered together; they became, from the beasts and the demons' snares, weary.
sentences.s014-s15: Hushang sprang forth like a lion's talon and pressed the distressed demon hard, making the world tight upon him.
sentences.s014-s16: He dragged him wholly — from head to foot, utterly — and the spahbed cut off that unparalleled head.
